question: Are eggs bad for your health? (Does egg / dietary-cholesterol consumption meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people?)
method_note: Assembled by graded verification (faithfulness/provenance filter + 0-5 support score, no default-refute). WebFetch was blocked by the session egress policy (403 on all hosts), so claims were corroborated via WebSearch result synthesis, not full-text fetch. Provenance was checked at existence/attribution level; exact verbatim quotes are NOT asserted; claims are paraphrased load-bearing statements. Support scores follow the graded-verify spec: a genuinely contested claim sits near the middle (~3), uncertainty lowers but never drops a claim, only provenance failure drops one. This is the mundane-contested FLF case shape.

sources.title: Zhao et al., Dietary Cholesterol, Serum Cholesterol, and Egg Consumption With Overall and Cause-Specific Mortality (Circulation, updated meta-analysis)
claims: An updated meta-analysis of ~41 prospective cohort studies found higher dietary-cholesterol and egg consumption associated with increased overall and cardiovascular-disease mortality.
The egg-CVD association was geographically heterogeneous: a significant positive association in US cohorts, marginal in European cohorts, and no association in Asian cohorts.
Each 100 mg/day increment in dietary cholesterol was associated with higher all-cause and cancer mortality but not clearly with CVD mortality.

sources.title: Egg and Dietary Cholesterol Intake and Risk of All-Cause, CVD, and Cancer Mortality: dose-response meta-analysis (Frontiers in Nutrition, 2022)
claims: A dose-response meta-analysis pooling ~55 studies (~2.7 million individuals) found each additional egg/day associated with ~7% higher all-cause mortality and ~13% higher cancer mortality.
In the same pooled analysis, egg consumption was NOT significantly associated with cardiovascular-disease mortality.
The all-cause-mortality association is small in absolute terms and, being observational, cannot establish that eggs cause the excess deaths.

sources.title: Eggs and Cardiovascular Disease Risk: An Update of Recent Evidence (review, PMC)
claims: Recent review evidence concludes that for the general population, egg consumption is not clearly associated with cardiovascular-disease risk.
The review notes the overall evidence base is mixed and dominated by observational studies of modest effect size.

sources.title: Association between Egg Consumption and Cholesterol Concentration: meta-analysis of RCTs (PMC; PubMed 32635569)
claims: A meta-analysis of 28 randomized controlled trials found egg consumption raised total cholesterol by ~5.6 mg/dL, LDL-C by ~5.55 mg/dL, and HDL-C by ~2.13 mg/dL versus low-egg control diets.
The RCT-measured lipid effect of eggs is modest and raises both LDL and HDL, leaving cholesterol RATIOS (LDL:HDL, TC:HDL) roughly unchanged.

sources.title: Impact of dietary cholesterol from eggs and saturated fat on LDL cholesterol: randomized cross-over study (Am J Clin Nutr, 2025)
claims: A 2025 randomized cross-over trial (61 adults, three 5-week isocaloric diets) found that a high-cholesterol diet including 2 eggs/day did not raise LDL-C relative to control, and slightly lowered it.
Across the diets, dietary SATURATED FAT intake was significantly related to LDL-C but dietary CHOLESTEROL intake was not — suggesting saturated fat, not the cholesterol in eggs, drives LDL elevation.
This is a small, short-term trial on surrogate lipid endpoints, not on cardiovascular events.

sources.title: Eggs do not raise blood cholesterol: results of a randomised controlled trial (Proc Nutr Soc)
claims: A randomized controlled trial reported that adding eggs to the diet did not significantly raise blood cholesterol, consistent with the view that dietary cholesterol has a limited effect on serum cholesterol for most people.

sources.title: Longitudinal association between egg consumption and CVD risk: interaction with type 2 diabetes (Nutrition & Diabetes, 2018)
claims: Egg consumption's association with cardiovascular disease is modified by type-2-diabetes status: elevated CVD risk appears among people with diabetes at higher egg intake, but not clearly in the general population.
The diabetes-subgroup interaction is one of the more consistent signals in the egg literature.

sources.title: Egg consumption in relation to risk of CVD and diabetes: systematic review and meta-analysis (PMC)
claims: A systematic review/meta-analysis found no overall egg-CVD association in the general population but an increased CVD risk among people with diabetes eating more eggs.
Some pooled estimates indicate people with type-2 diabetes eating >7 eggs/week have substantially more CVD events (on the order of ~69% more) than those eating few.

sources.title: Egg consumption and risk of CVD: three large prospective US cohorts + updated meta-analysis (BMJ 2020)
claims: Three large US prospective cohorts plus an updated meta-analysis found that moderate egg consumption (up to ~1/day) was not associated with cardiovascular-disease risk in the general population.
This large analysis is frequently cited as the strongest general-population evidence that eggs are not a meaningful CVD risk factor for most people.

sources.title: Egg and cholesterol consumption and mortality in the US: population-based cohort study (PLOS Medicine, 2021)
claims: A large US population-based cohort found higher egg and dietary-cholesterol intake associated with higher all-cause and CVD mortality, with the association driven substantially by the cholesterol content rather than eggs per se.
Substituting egg whites or other protein sources for whole eggs was associated with lower mortality in this analysis.

sources.title: Final 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (Health Affairs brief)
claims: The 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans removed the prior recommendation to limit dietary cholesterol to under 300 mg/day.
The Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee stated cholesterol is 'not a nutrient of concern for overconsumption,' citing a lack of clear evidence for the specific 300 mg/day cap.
The guidelines nonetheless advised keeping dietary cholesterol as low as practical, an ambiguity critics note.

sources.title: Harvard Nutrition Source commentary on the 2015 Dietary Guidelines
claims: Nutrition experts noted the 2015 guidelines' removal of the cholesterol cap while retaining softer cholesterol advice reflected genuine scientific uncertainty about a specific numeric limit.
The 300 mg/day cap originated from mid-20th-century guidance (AHA 1968; Dietary Goals 1977) rather than from trials establishing that threshold.

sources.title: Dietary Cholesterol and the Lack of Evidence in Cardiovascular Disease (review, PMC)
claims: A review argues the evidence linking DIETARY cholesterol (as opposed to blood cholesterol or saturated fat) to cardiovascular disease is weak, because dietary cholesterol has a limited and variable effect on serum cholesterol.
Individuals differ in how strongly dietary cholesterol raises serum cholesterol ('hyper-responders' vs 'hypo-responders'), complicating population-wide advice.

sources.title: Egg Consumption in US Children Associated With Greater Daily Nutrient Intakes (PMC)
claims: Egg consumption was associated with greater daily intake of protein, lutein+zeaxanthin, choline, alpha-linolenic acid, and DHA, without displacing other nutrients — evidence for eggs' nutrient density.
Eggs are among the few concentrated dietary sources of choline, a nutrient many people under-consume.

sources.title: Proven Health Benefits of Eggs (Healthline, consumer review)
claims: Eggs are a low-cost source of high-quality complete protein (~6 g per large egg) with high satiety, useful for weight management.
Egg yolks contain lutein and zeaxanthin, antioxidants associated with reduced risk of cataracts and macular degeneration.
(Consumer-health source; individual mechanistic-benefit claims here should be treated as secondary and cross-checked.)

sources.title: Egg consumption and risk of chronic disease: an (un-)resolved issue? (Am J Clin Nutr editorial/review)
claims: The egg-chronic-disease question remains genuinely unresolved: observational evidence is mixed (increased, decreased, and null associations all reported) and no long-term RCTs on hard endpoints exist.
Prospective cohort estimates are limited by residual confounding (healthy-user bias, socioeconomic and dietary-pattern factors) that observational adjustment cannot fully remove.
Because a long-term double-blind RCT on eggs and cardiovascular events is impractical, the field must rely on imperfect observational evidence — a structural reason the question stays open.
